item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While the emails at the end of the SCP kind of fall flat for me, what this piece manages to flesh out with the sanitarium/asylum more than makes up for it. Being involuntarily hospitalized for a (although fairly brief) time, this particularly resonates with me and gets under my skin in a delightfully horrific way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As far as Browning is concerned…

…isn't that what the Ethics Commission is for?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You say you hope they got out? I don't, I know they did. I just hope they got out alive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Knowing as much as I do about the sordid history of psychology and psychiatry (it's BETTER these days, I swear it, though there are still issues), this hit a suitably creepy tone with me. So many stories of wrongful hospitalizations, abhorrent treatment, refusal to release because having patients was profitable, the extreme rarity of anyone getting out…it's frightening that the only anomalous part of this is that the hospital isn't on this plane of existence, because everything else actually happened to people.

insert self-congratulatory wankery here

Here's to another year of desperately trying to jailbreak our psychological source-code to allow for some sort of happiness before the deranged software developer that is Life notices his evolutionary algorithm accidentally produced sapience, leading him to patch that bug in the next update.

Cheers!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This doesn't have the imagery or even punchiness that I expected it to have, but despite that, it really, really worked for me. Unhesitant +1

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Just to clarify/expedite things, the staff can presume my pre-approval for any crosslink along these lines (any crosslinks that amount to the anomaly having 'vanished' Foundation personnel who have been documented as mentally ill).

An alternative approach that would also work, and would help emphasize the theme this anomaly is meant to embody — replace this line in the containment procedures:

No personnel with a documented history of mental illness are permitted on-site.

With this:

No personnel with a documented history of mental illness are permitted on-site. Psychiatric counseling for those assigned to SCP-3054 is to remain unofficial; neither a diagnosis nor any documentation of these sessions is to be made. Personnel who have had prior contact with anomalies associated with mental illness (such as X, Y, and Z) are to undergo heightened scrutiny to ensure they have not been officially diagnosed with a condition present in any edition of the DSM (Diagnostic and Statistics Manual of Mental Disorders).

Wherein 'X', 'Y', and 'Z' can be any mental-affecting SCPs you want. It kind of kills the nice simplicity of that one-line hook, but — to be honest — the more I write SCPs, the less interested in one-line hooks I become. Also, I like how this now emphasizes the central theme (that it's being officially diagnosed that makes you vulnerable).

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
I wouldn't be surprised if Bart Hughes didn't have any diagnosed obsessive, paranoid, cognitive or memory disorder given his work.

Mary Nakayama tried to kill herself as a teenager and probably had sexual assault related PTSD.

The people who end up as the caretakers of SCP-2508 often seem troubled and the O5-O7 who wrote the excerpt seems like they might have been alcoholic.

There are probably dozens of examples, working for The Foundation almost certainly results in extensive psychiatric treatment, and characters dissappearing is probably only slightly less common then their bodies being recovered.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: I definitely approve of the new crosslink suggestion described in the timer (if only because I'd like to see SCP-3241 get more love).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Lord, I thought the logs were brutal enough—though they were tempered somewhat by the fact that I could tell early on where things were heading.

But that first email? That was a hell of a gut punch. That's the icing on the horrific cake. Great job setting things up so the reader almost feels like they're the ones being gaslighted. It's only once in a blue moon that a skip actually makes me feel queasy, but this did it for me.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: Hmm, do you know of any?

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: A stronger crosslink might be through an existing character from another SCP or tale, with a documented mental illness, who could have (or did) mysteriously disappeared, and therefore could be considered as a possible victim of SCP-3054 (which can work even there's meta-knowledge that they can't be)?

item.title: Belated
item.content:encoded: I thought I had eventually upvoted, but apparently I didn't. Anyway this definitely deserves it. Thank you for listening to my criticism.

item.title: Regrettably, I'd have to veto.
item.content:encoded:
I really dislike saying no to these things; I love using parts of my skips as an opportunity to highlight the existence of other skips (particularly ones that deserve more exposure). I also don't want to discourage the tremendous effort and work the cross-link team is doing! That being said:

It has recently been discovered that any person with a documented history of mental illness may experience SCP-3054 while inside of SCP-3054-A (see below).

This is a very important detail in the piece, and one that often gets missed. It isn't having mental illness that triggers its effect; it's being *documented* as having a mental illness that triggers it. You could be 100% healthy, but mistakenly documented as schizophrenic, and the effect triggers; you could be an undiagnosed schizophrenic, and the effect *wouldn't* trigger, because there's no documentation of your mental illness.

So anomalies that make you mentally ill wouldn't matter; only being documented as mentally ill would matter.

This is incredibly important because part of the anxiety I wanted to explore here is the stigma of being documented as mentally ill, and how your actual mental state is irrelevant to our mental health system. That's one of the central themes of the piece: Mental illness is less about your mental state and more about what the system has defined your mental state as being.

The crosslink suggested here would, regrettably, directly contradict that theme.

item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: author approved edits

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

This skip mentions Director Browning, who is a central character in SCP-3241. I propose crosslinking to that skip.

Editing this:

FROM: Director Browning <noitadnuof.pcs|gninworbb#noitadnuof.pcs|gninworbb>

To this:

FROM: Director Browning <noitadnuof.pcs|gninworbb#noitadnuof.pcs|gninworbb>

Also noting that, since this is pretty much a new WikiWalk proposal, I've restarted the timer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this scp so far has been both the most disturbing thing I’ve read and the best ending I’ve ever read.

item.title: Re: Time period problem
item.content:encoded: Oops; pardon. I'll have to think of a few things (date-wise) to fix this issue, but I'll take care of it later today. Thanks for pointing it out!

item.title: Time period problem
item.content:encoded: "Flappy Bird was released in May 2013 and gained its popularity in early 2014"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't make a lesbian scientist pissed off, or you will eat some fresh grenades.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yet another good one I completely missed seeing. D: Good thing you wrote that tale about it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've had a few narrow misses, and have family that weren't so fortunate. Painfully realistic situation, conveyed with all the disgustingly stacked cards you have to play with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was a gripping read from start to finish. I'm a little jealous that I can't do a VO of it because I am a man and would not do justice impersonating the female protagonists. But if I could record it, I would, because I really enjoyed it. So good job, take my +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is stunning, i nearly cried. hope the two of them made it out, or at least found each other before the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love every bit of it! Great build up and that node from Lindsey was awesome!

+1 all the way :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Possibly TMI, possibly disturbing (regarding experiences in hospitals with inability to leave), hence spoiler'd:
+ show block
– hide block

The creepiest part to me about this sort of thing is how oblivious so many doctors seem to be toward it. Like, I've watched doctors respond with complete bafflement when a patient who was involuntarily committed expressed a desire to leave. I remember one doctor's words very clearly: "But why do you want to leave? You're clearly unwell, you need to stay here and get better!"

Lady, if you don't understand why someone doesn't want to stay in the place where they were dragged against their will — taken away from everything comfortable and familiar — then forced to sleep in a room full of strangers, dosed with daily sedatives, given slop to eat, allowed to speak with the people they love for only a few hours each week, and told they can't leave until they fulfill whatever arbitrary requirements for sanity you've set forth — then I think you're the one who needs help.

Even those extreme circumstances aside, though — the failure for so many doctors (in my experience — others may vary!) to understand how terrifying a place like this can be is, in my mind, partly what makes places like this so terrifying. A lot of the staff I saw just don't seem to understand how frightening it is to be in a place like this — how easy it is to think you're never going to get out — particularly when you're already in an emotionally vulnerable state.

And it's not like this is even an "irrational" fear. In a lot of ways, mental health patients have less rights than prison inmates. Wardens can't arbitrarily decide to make you stay because they don't feel you've been fully 'rehabilitated'. Psychiatrists most certainly can.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was in a psych ward back in January or so, and even though it was for something relatively (relative to more long term mental health conditions, that is) minor, and even though I didn't "feel" mentally ill (there's some saying or somesuch about if you can question if you're insane, you must be sane), there was this constant and continuous sense of dread that there was something more wrong with me than even I realized, that somehow they would see something I didn't and my stay would be extended indefinitely, and that I'd be there for who knows how long for reasons beyond my control. This SCP did a pretty solid job of dredging up that fear and sense of constantly-fluctuating hope/hopelessness (hope I'll get out, hopeless because what if I can't, why bother?) that kept me on edge during my brief stay. So, in summary, +1, and I may hate you a little (not really) for taking me back to that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oops, I forgot about that — thanks, fixed!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why is her email slindsey if her first name is june? wouldn't it be jlindsey?

Browning's dickishness now sounds more plausible. Sirpudding's critique was kind of harsh, but it has a bit more versimiltude to it now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've never been in the system, but under different circumstances, it's extremely likely that I could have been. And this got to me.

Take my emphatic upvote, and know that in the version of this story that plays out in my head, there's a happy ending.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just about cried reading this. Won't elaborate too much; just take my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, it doesn't care if you're actually suffering from mental illness (that's partly what I wanted to get across with Lindsey's method of gaining entrance). All it cares about is that someone, somewhere, at some point officially decided that you are mentally ill. And put it in writing.

Which is kind of the point, because — that's one of the fucked up things in psychiatry this thing represents.

I'm sorry that it made you sick to your stomach! It's kind of supposed to (obviously!), but I don't want to make people feel too much like crap (I was hoping the ending might alleviate some of that, but it's still pretty messed up stuff, I know). Thank you for the feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Even more important: The diagnosis of schizophrenia may have actually been completely wrong!

So, this thing doesn't actually operate on whether or not the person is actually mentally ill, just whether or not someone has put in writing that they're mentally ill (or autistic, or whatnot)? The skip made me sick to my stomach before, this addition does not help my feelings right now.

I mean, great job? I'm pretty sure my reaction was what you were going for here! You really hit me in the gut there. But I'm not going back and reading this again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Without that spatial manipulation stuff in the beginning, it makes the moment when she ends right back up at the front doors just a liiiiiittle bit harder to see coming.

That's why I accepted the use of shifting geography here. It played a part in the resolution (even if it was a predictable part) rather than simply being tacked-on for the sake of it. Considering that this anomaly is in an alternate dimension/reality though, the ending is still believable and fits the narrative themes without it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Jesus. This is the first SCP that's gotten to me in a while. Easy +1, although I think the ending wraps things up a little too nicely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(Continuing the conversation in PMs, but just wanted to mention here)

After some thought, I've made several significant changes to the letter from Director Browning. I don't agree with all of your points (sirpudding), but I agree with enough of them to prompt some rewriting.

(Also, I again apologize for taking your tone to be hostile; I think this piece is a little personal for me, so I'm maybe a bit sensitive about it!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've been thinking about this, and I think you might be spot-on. I think I'm going to edit it to remove references to shifting geography, or waking up in a different bed:

+ show block
– hide block

The only reasons I added that in was to get across that the building contains way more people than is immediately obvious… but I think seeing the different faces every day gets that across just as well.

(Also, I want this place to be as non-anomalous as possible. The horror isn't that it shifts around, or that the place is spooky; that makes it seem way more interesting than it is. The horror is its banality — its sameness — that this is where you spend the rest of your life. A series of faces you'll never recognize, living in a fog without hope, waiting to die. Enormous stretches of crippling, crushing boredom and tedium punctuated by tiny, acute bursts of terror, paternalism, and cruelty. The shifting geography and waking-up-in-a-new-bed don't really add to that horror, I don't think, and therefore should probably be cut. I can still have her bring up the different faces, which can still be used against her in front of the judge.)

And on a final note: Without that spatial manipulation stuff in the beginning, it makes the moment when she ends right back up at the front doors just a liiiiiittle bit harder to see coming.


EDIT: Yep, made the edit, it was easy to take out — and I think it improves the story.

Thank you for bringing this up!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've once had a dream of a mental institution. It was hazy, as lucidness usually is, but quite more vivid than is often with me. A large brick style building surrounded by twilight where the staff were faceless and the admitted always stricken with fear. I remember seeing needles. Injections that left patients for all intents and purposes clinically dead or worse.

This article brought that memory back to me, and for that reason among others, I absolutely love it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is suitably nightmarish. It strikes me as a twist on the Unreliable Narrator. There's also an echo of the Rosenhan Experiment, except that the facility is unreachable and unwilling to release anyone. Maybe unable to release anyone? Where would they even be, were they to be released?
This is much more frightening than a generic Thing What Kills You could ever be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm settling on an upvote. The logs develop nicely and are immersive. However, it is also fairly predictable in terms of the way in which Owens progresses as a character, as well as the resolution. The notion of a place with a constantly shifting internal layout has been done before on the site, and this doesn't offer anything new in that regard. Nevertheless, it is a well-crafted yarn.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm kind of not feeling this whole conversation; it's giving me a super-hostile vibe.

I apologize, I don't intend any hostility. I really don't like Browning, but I gather that was your intention, so that is fine. I just don't find the email itself very plausible.

It's okay that you downvoted the article, I don't mind that you dislike it

I don't dislike it. I really liked it, up until that email, which kills it for me.

I'm mostly just responding to what feels, to me, like a lot of misconceptions about schizophrenia, mental health, and mental health records in the US.

Yes I understand that the records of minors are sealed, or often even destroyed. I think you have some misconceptions about how background checks work. For high level clearances they don't just rely on public records and self-reporting. They actually talk to people who know you. If literally no one living exists who knows her, they simply wouldn't be able to clear her.

If you want to continue this conversation, then maybe we could do it on PMs, or in IRC? Maybe I'd feel more comfortable there?

I don't really like taking critique to PMs. It doesn't really help anybody else and is therefore kind of pointless. If you PM me, I will respond though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

I… okay, I think there's a lot of levels of miscommunication going on here?

  • The records may literally not exist anymore.
  • Parents may decide not to tell you they were in the facility. And, aside from their doctors (who might not remember them), their parents may genuinely be the only people who knew (plus, they might be dead! Or otherwise unavailable to discuss!). There's even circumstances where parents themselves wouldn't know (I know that sounds bizarre, but I can elaborate in PM if you'd like).
  • People with schizophrenia don't always take medication for their schizophrenia, so it's not like you can 'track' it that way.
  • Schizophrenia doesn't always show up on psychological screenings anyway (Again, speaking from experience, here)
  • Even more important: The diagnosis of schizophrenia may have actually been completely wrong! (I've seen that happen, too)

Way less important, but:

  • Her phone tracks to SCP-XXXX-A. It's there, just in another dimension.
  • He wants to find out what the hell this dimension is (and if there's a way to come back from it) before he starts sending armed soldiers into it?

I'm kind of not feeling this whole conversation; it's giving me a super-hostile vibe. It's okay that you downvoted the article, I don't mind that you dislike it — I'm mostly just responding to what feels, to me, like a lot of misconceptions about schizophrenia, mental health, and mental health records in the US.

If you want to continue this conversation, then maybe we could do it on PMs, or in IRC? Maybe I'd feel more comfortable there?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sirpudding did raise a good point, but since I didn't notice until after it was pointed out, I'll let it slide. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
sirpudding, I don't think you know much about how mental health records work?

I don't think that you know how background checks for security clearances work. They don't need her sealed records (although they can require you to provide them yourself), because they will have interviewed her parents, teachers and so on. Furthermore, this is for legal background checks and the Foundation isn't bound by law.

They certainly would have asked her if she had a history of mental illness, and she would have agreed under oath that the information that she gave was true and complete.

Besides this isn't addressing the problem of psychological screening. If she is taking medication for schizophrenia, the Foundation would certainly know, and if she is an untreated schizophrenic they would probably have diagnosed this. The Foundation has an interest in the mental health of employees, even more so than real life espionage agencies do. Not only can a Foundation employee leak, but they can also be possessed, mind controlled, infected with anomalous memes, ect.

If you took away a different conclusion than that, then maybe I need to reassess the email itself?

He comes across as extremely dismissive and the impression that I got was that he thinks she is somewhere totally mundane and delusional and because of this isn't going to do anything about it. He could track her phone. He could insert an MTF to recover her. He almost certainly should call the Foundation equivalent of a DUST-ONE (which should be mandatory). He could do a lot of things, but instead he sends an email to her supervisor indicating he doesn't give a shit about the people under his command.

(Also, I don't think the Foundation terminates people for incompetence, but that might just be a difference of opinion!)

The Foundation is an extralegal organization, and effectively a massive (and impossibly successful) criminal conspiracy. They can't just fire people for the same reason mobs can't just fire people. The options seem to be either kill them or make them into amnesiac vegetables (and the latter assumes a confidence in the effectiveness of amnestics that seems implausible).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

sirpudding, I don't think you know much about how mental health records work? I can go into more detail if you'd like via PM — but suffice to say — yes, it's perfectly possible a screening by the NSA would not reveal that you were in a mental health clinic for schizophrenia as a teenager.

Re: Browning's assessment, that isn't what I wanted the reader to take away; he's definitely taking it seriously, and he's definitely planning on running some tests (by having people enter SCP-XXXX through SCP-XXXX-A with video equipment and more, for example) to confirm her report. He just doesn't want to update the article before getting additional confirmation.

If you took away a different conclusion than that, then maybe I need to reassess the email itself? I'll give it some thought.

(Also, I don't think the Foundation terminates people for incompetence, but that might just be a difference of opinion!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They'd probably just do your standard screening and call it a day.

The standard screening for something like Top Secret Clearance at the NSA would certainly uncover this kind of thing. The Foundation is even more covert than that. If you lie about your background to the NSA and they find out later, you go to Federal prison, but if you lie to the Foundation about your background and they find out later you end up a Jane Doe in an amnesia ward or an unmarked grave.

If she really is schizophrenic, then that really ought to be diagnosable, or she is being treated, either way it seems nigh-unbelievably incompetent for the Foundation to have given her a Level 2 clearance at minimum, exposed her to the paranormal, and not have known.

He's not doubting she was affected by SCP-3054; he's merely doubting that her description of alt-dimension Cragstaff was accurate.

He lost a researcher, who is probably being held by entities with unknown abilities and motives, and isn't treating this a a severe security breach (which is almost certainly policy). He isn't apparently even willing to take extremely basic measures to confirm her report. He should be fired (and probably terminated) for gross incompetence.

He also isn't showing the least amount of responsibility or concern for the people under his command, which isn't implausible, some leaders are just bad in any large organization, but if I was on the O5, I would probably want to put the bullet in him myself after reading this…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Regarding Julia's mental health records:
+ show block
– hide block

Julia went in as a teenager. Speaking from real life experience: Tracking down mental health history records for someone back when they were a teenager can be absurdly hard, if not literally impossible. And that's when the person in question gives you permission! It gets even more ridiculous if the mental health institute has since closed. Harder still if you don't know what you're looking for. And on top of all that, states have different requirements for how long they're required to keep these documents — they may have already been destroyed!

I imagine that, yeah — the Foundation has the resources to even retrieve documents which no longer physically exist — but they're not going to pull those resources out to screen every researcher they hire. They'd probably just do your standard screening and call it a day.

Lindsey dug way deeper — largely because she had good reason to.

And as for Browning's assessment:
+ show block
– hide block

He's not saying to discount her testimony, he's just being a jerk and pointing out that the logs speak for themselves. He doesn't want to update the article to include direct mention of 'alt-dimension' Cragstaff because Julia's depiction is anecdotal; he brings up the schizophrenia mostly just because he's a fucking asshole.

He's not doubting she was taken by SCP-3054; he's merely doubting that her description of alt-dimension Cragstaff was accurate. Maybe that wasn't clear from his email? I don't know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was prepared to upvote until the email from Director Browning. I can't really imagine that the Foundation doesn't do background checks or psychological screening. Also there's really no believable way that a Site Director (?) would ever assume that a missing researcher, reporting a kidnapping by unknown paranormal agency, is just having a schizophrenic episode. This is a security risk, of the kind that should require a massive organizational crisis response, and the Foundation has no reason to be skeptical of this, why don't they at least confirm it by tracking her phone?

Prior to that this was tense and scary as hell though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loved it in chat, love it here. All the +1s.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
And if the cloud bursts, thunder in your ear,
You shout, and no one seems to hear --
And if the band you're in starts playing different tunes --

-- I'll see you on the dark side of the moon ~

SOURCES:

  • Images here and here; both public domain.

FEEDBACK:

  • Bentu ([SCP-3148] - Tranquility)
  • Zel
  • edwardbuck117
  • spacefin
  • Tufto ([SCP-3799] - A Short History of Snowfall)
  • All the FaiDites who helped (you know who you are).
AUTHOR NOTES (warning: drama)
You've been warned!

This story is a little bit personal, and for that I apologize. It's based on some actual shit I've dealt with and seen (particularly the bits with the judge). I've never been admitted into a place like this, but I've been in Lindsey's position enough times in my life to get a vague notion of what's going on inside them.

I'm definitely not here to start up a conversation about psychiatry in America — and my heart goes out to anyone working in the field (it's certainly a thankless task, and I've meant enough wonderful psychiatrists to know they exist, they're saving lives, and they need as much support as we can bear to provide them with). But some of these places fucking terrify me. In part, I wrote this SCP to try and show you why.

There's no final twist, no skeleton in the closet, no 'they were ghosts all along!'. Cragstaff is just all the misery I've seen in these places poured into one horrible fucking hole. And that hole will never, ever let you leave.

I have no idea if the two of them made it out. I hope they did.

Like the article? Click here for more of The Great Hippo's Skippos!
